display_name: A BGP route present but not installed is usually an unreachable next-hop, not a missing route
context: A prefix appears in the BGP table but is not selected as best (no '>' marker) and is missing from the routing table
observation: show bgp shows the prefix with a valid path but without the best-path marker, and the route is absent from the RIB/FIB
naive_interpretation: BGP is not receiving the route, or there is a route filtering / advertisement problem upstream
correct_interpretation: The route is being received — the issue is that its BGP next-hop is not resolvable in the IGP/RIB, so BGP marks the path invalid and cannot select it as best. This is extremely common with iBGP, where the next-hop is the original eBGP peer address and the receiving router has no route to it (the classic fix is next-hop-self on the iBGP edge). The route exists; it is unusable because its next-hop has no recursive resolution.
why: RFC 4271 requires the NEXT_HOP be resolvable for a path to be considered valid and eligible for best-path selection
iBGP does not change the next-hop by default, so an internal router often receives a next-hop it has no IGP route to
An unresolved next-hop makes the path 'inaccessible'/'not synchronized' and excludes it from best-path, so it never reaches the RIB
The presence of the prefix in the BGP table proves advertisement worked — the failure is in next-hop reachability, a different layer

confirmation_commands.purpose: Show the path detail including the next-hop and whether it is marked valid/inaccessible
Verify the BGP next-hop actually resolves in the IGP/RIB — the root check
Confirm the path is 'not best' due to 'inaccessible' next-hop rather than filtering

traps: Chasing a route-advertisement or filtering problem when the prefix is plainly present but next-hop-unresolvable
Forgetting that iBGP keeps the eBGP next-hop, so internal routers need next-hop-self or an IGP route to that next-hop
Not actually resolving the next-hop address in the RIB before theorizing about the cause
Confusing 'not best' (a usable competing path lost) with 'invalid' (next-hop unreachable) — different reasons a route stays out of the RIB
